stem:
A 5-week-old first-born male presents with a 6-day history of vomiting that started as small post-feed possets and has become forceful after every feed over the last 48 hours. His mother describes the vomit as just looking like milk. He has been feeding hungrily between vomits. Today she noted his nappies are noticeably less wet. On a test feed in ED, a 1.5 cm firm mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant of his abdomen.

Blood results are now back from the ED point-of-care analyser and he is awaiting a paediatric surgical opinion.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 165/min, blood pressure 78/45 mmHg, respiratory rate 38/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Sunken anterior fontanelle, dry mucous membranes; abdomen soft with palpable RUQ mass during test feed; visible gastric peristalsis
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 134 mmol/L; potassium 3.0 mmol/L; chloride 90 mmol/L; bicarbonate 32 mmol/L; pH 7.51; glucose 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Proceed directly to pyloromyotomy after a single 20 mL/kg saline bolus is delivered in the emergency department.
options.rationale: Operating on an alkalotic, hypokalaemic infant risks postoperative apnoea and is the anti-pattern to recognise. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Trial a three day course of oral erythromycin ethylsuccinate 12.5 mg/kg given four times daily.
options.rationale: Erythromycin is used in some non-Australian centres for early prokinetic effect but is not the AU first-line and risks pyloric stenosis itself in young infants. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Commence 0.9 percent saline with added potassium and refer the infant for definitive surgical repair.
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is medical first then surgical: correct the hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis before pyloromyotomy. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Recommend thickened feeds with ranitidine 1 mg/kg given three times daily and outpatient review next week.
options.rationale: Thickened feeds and acid suppression treat gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, which does not produce a palpable pyloric mass and biochemical alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Arrange an upper gastrointestinal contrast study before any fluid resuscitation to confirm the diagnosis.
options.rationale: When a pyloric mass is palpable on test feed and biochemistry fits, imaging is for confirmation but must not delay fluid and electrolyte correction. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically gives projectile non-bilious vomiting between weeks 3 and 8, a hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses, and a palpable olive in the right upper quadrant. Surgery is curative but must be deferred until chloride is at least 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate is below 28 mmol/L, and potassium has been replaced, because alkalosis predisposes to postoperative central apnoea. Initial management is 0.9% saline with potassium at 1.5 to 2 times maintenance for rehydration.
key_points: Treat the alkalosis before the operation: Cl above 100, HCO3 below 28, K corrected.
0.9 percent saline with KCl is the resuscitation fluid of choice.
Persistent alkalosis predisposes to postoperative apnoea.
explanation.pearls: If chloride is below 90 mmol/L on arrival, expect 24 to 48 hours of fluid correction before anaesthesia is safe; book theatre but resist pressure to operate early.

stem:
An 8-day-old, previously well term breastfed male becomes acutely unwell at home with three vomits in the last 4 hours that his mother describes as bright green. He has not had a wet nappy in the last 6 hours and has not opened his bowels today. On arrival to the emergency department he is mottled, sleepy but rousable, and grunting intermittently. His abdomen is soft but slightly distended with reduced bowel sounds and no palpable mass.

Intravenous access has been secured and bloods are being processed; the surgical registrar has been paged.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 180/min, blood pressure 65/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 50/min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.4°C
data_table.Examination: Mottled, sleepy but rousable; capillary refill 4 seconds; abdomen soft, slightly distended; no palpable mass or hernia
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.27, lactate 3.8 mmol/L; bedside glucose 3.6 mmol/L; bloods pending

lead_in: After initial resuscitation, what is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Erect abdominal radiograph plus a left lateral decubitus film to look for free intra-abdominal air.
options.rationale: A normal AXR does not exclude malrotation with midgut volvulus and free air implies perforation, which is a late finding. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal bilious vomiting)

options.text: Ultrasound of the abdomen looking for the target sign of an ileocolic intussusception.
options.rationale: Intussusception classically presents between 3 and 12 months of age and is rare in the first 2 weeks of life. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Stool culture with rotavirus PCR together with rehydration and observation in the short stay unit.
options.rationale: Gastroenteritis does not typically cause bilious vomiting in a previously well neonate, and observation risks bowel infarction. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal bilious vomiting)

options.text: Upper gastrointestinal contrast study after a 10 mL/kg saline bolus and ongoing maintenance fluid.
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with volvulus until disproved, and upper GI contrast is the diagnostic test of choice. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal bilious vomiting)

options.text: Per rectal contrast enema to look for meconium ileus or another distal bowel obstruction.
options.rationale: Contrast enema evaluates distal obstruction but does not show malrotation, the most time-critical diagnosis here. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal bilious vomiting)

explanation.summary: Bile-stained vomiting in any neonate carries malrotation with midgut volvulus on the differential until imaging excludes it, because untreated volvulus leads to bowel ischaemia within hours. Upper gastrointestinal contrast study is the first-line investigation, looking for an abnormal duodenojejunal flexure position to the right of the midline or a corkscrew appearance. Resuscitation, nil by mouth, nasogastric decompression, and urgent paediatric surgical involvement precede or run alongside imaging.
key_points: Bilious vomit equals malrotation until proven otherwise.
Upper GI contrast is the diagnostic test of choice.
Resuscitate and call surgery before imaging delays definitive care.
explanation.pearls: If the upper GI cannot be obtained within an hour at your site, transfer arrangements to a paediatric surgical centre must start immediately rather than waiting for imaging.

stem:
A 4-year-old previously well boy is on day 6 of a fluctuating fever that peaks at 39.8°C and is poorly responsive to paracetamol. His parents describe his eyes as red without any discharge for three days, a strawberry tongue from day 4, a polymorphous truncal rash since day 4, and recently noted redness and swelling of his hands. He has a single 1.8 cm tender anterior cervical lymph node on the right. There are no oral vesicles. He has had no contact with anyone known to have COVID-19; nasopharyngeal SARS-CoV-2 PCR returned negative this morning.

A blood panel and echocardiogram have just been completed by the cardiology fellow.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 140/min, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 30/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 39.2°C
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, polymorphous truncal rash, swollen erythematous hands, single 1.8 cm right cervical node; no oral vesicles
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.5 x10^9/L; CRP 165 mg/L; ESR 78 mm/hr; platelets 480 x10^9/L; Hb 95 g/L (110-140); ALT 90 U/L; albumin 28 g/L (35-50); sodium 131 mmol/L; urine 30 leukocytes per HPF, no organisms; echocardiogram shows LMCA z-score +2.1, RCA z-score +2.4, small pericardial effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg IV daily for three days, withholding IVIG until day 10 of fever.
options.rationale: Steroid monotherapy is not first-line in Australian practice and IVIG should not be delayed when criteria are met. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease statement)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg plus IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg pending blood culture results overnight.
options.rationale: Empirical sepsis treatment alone misses the IVIG window and does not address coronary inflammation. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Anakinra 2 mg/kg/day given subcutaneously as monotherapy because the criteria are only partial.
options.rationale: Anakinra is reserved for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease, not initial therapy. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease statement)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily plus aspirin 5 mg/kg daily as antiplatelet cover.
options.rationale: Low-dose aspirin with prednisolone alone fails to address acute coronary inflammation and is insufficient initial therapy. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease statement)

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg infused over 12 hours plus oral aspirin 30 mg/kg/day in four divided doses.
options.rationale: Incomplete Kawasaki criteria are met with persistent fever, three principal features, supportive labs, and coronary z-scores at or above 2.0, mandating IVIG plus high-dose aspirin. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease statement)

explanation.summary: This child meets incomplete Kawasaki criteria: fever for at least 5 days with three of five principal clinical features and supplementary laboratory findings (anaemia for age, albumin below 30 g/L, ALT elevation, platelets above 450, WCC above 15, sterile pyuria, sodium below 135) plus coronary z-scores at or above 2.0 to 2.5. A negative SARS-CoV-2 PCR with classic Kawasaki phenotype argues against multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children. Treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg/day divided four-hourly until afebrile 48 hours, then 3 to 5 mg/kg daily for at least 6 weeks.
key_points: Coronary z-score at or above 2.0 with three principal features supports incomplete KD.
IVIG 2 g/kg plus high-dose aspirin remains first-line in Australian practice.
IVIG resistance defined by persistent fever 36 hours post-infusion triggers a second IVIG dose or anakinra.
explanation.pearls: Hyponatraemia and a sterile pyuria are easily missed supplementary criteria that tilt a fever-of-unknown-origin towards incomplete Kawasaki when the principal features are partial.

stem:
A 32-year-old G2P1 woman at 27+5 weeks gestation attends her local diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic for her routine screening 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She is asymptomatic. Her booking weight gave a BMI of 29 kg/m2, she gained 4 kg in her last pregnancy with a 3.8 kg baby and no GDM diagnosed retrospectively, and she has no family history of type 2 diabetes. Her previous antenatal visits have been unremarkable. She fasted from midnight as instructed.

Her test results have just come back to the clinic registrar.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 78/min, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 28 cm; foetal heart 142/min; no oedema
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT: fasting glucose 5.3 mmol/L; 1 hour 11.4 mmol/L; 2 hour 8.0 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation and immediate management?

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes on the 1 hour value and start carbohydrate-counted dietary advice with home glucose monitoring.
options.rationale: ADIPS diagnostic thresholds for GDM on 75 g OGTT are fasting at or above 5.1, 1 hour at or above 10.0, or 2 hour at or above 8.5; one abnormal value makes the diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS 2014 guideline)

options.text: Report the test as normal and repeat screening only if foetal macrosomia develops on subsequent ultrasound.
options.rationale: The 1 hour value of 11.4 exceeds the ADIPS threshold of 10.0 mmol/L, so the test is diagnostic of GDM. (Source: ADIPS 2014 guideline)

options.text: Repeat the entire OGTT at 32 weeks to confirm the diagnosis before instituting any treatment changes.
options.rationale: A single OGTT meeting ADIPS thresholds is diagnostic; routine repeat testing for confirmation is not recommended. (Source: ADIPS 2014 guideline)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily immediately without trialling lifestyle modification first.
options.rationale: First-line initial therapy is dietary modification with glucose monitoring; pharmacotherapy is added if targets are not met. (Source: ADIPS 2014 guideline)

options.text: Order an HbA1c above 6.5 percent threshold to confirm gestational diabetes before any further action.
options.rationale: HbA1c is not the diagnostic test for GDM in Australia; the OGTT is the standard, and HbA1c can be misleading in pregnancy. (Source: ADIPS 2014 guideline)

explanation.summary: Australasian Diabetes in Pregnancy Society diagnostic thresholds on the 75 g OGTT are: fasting at or above 5.1 mmol/L, 1 hour at or above 10.0 mmol/L, or 2 hour at or above 8.5 mmol/L. A single abnormal value confirms gestational diabetes. Initial management is dietary modification, regular physical activity, and self-monitored blood glucose four times a day (fasting and one or two hours post-meal). Metformin or insulin is added if more than 20% of values exceed targets over a 7 to 14 day review.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds: fasting at or above 5.1, 1 hour at or above 10.0, 2 hour at or above 8.5 mmol/L.
First-line treatment is diet plus monitoring; pharmacotherapy if glucose targets are missed.
explanation.pearls: Use ADIPS, not the WHO thresholds, in Australian practice; the 1 hour 10.0 threshold is more stringent than the WHO 1 hour 10.0 but identical, and the fasting 5.1 is lower than the older 5.5.

stem:
A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 28+0 weeks gestation attends her routine antenatal visit at a metropolitan public hospital. She is well with no bleeding, abdominal pain, or recent abdominal trauma. Her booking blood group at 12 weeks was O Rh D-negative with a negative antibody screen; her partner is known to be Rh D-positive. She has had no sensitising events during this pregnancy. Foetal movements are felt regularly and there has been normal foetal growth on her morphology scan.

The antibody screen and group repeat performed at this visit have just returned.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 76/min, blood pressure 112/68 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 28 cm; foetal heart 148/min; abdomen soft and non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Group O Rh D-negative; antibody screen negative; foetal RhD genotyping not performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold anti-D until 34 weeks because the 28 week dose is not part of routine Australian protocol.
options.rationale: Australian guidelines have recommended dual 28 and 34 week dosing since 2003; withholding is incorrect. (Source: NBA anti-D guidelines)

options.text: Administer Rh D immunoglobulin 625 IU intramuscularly today and again at 34 weeks.
options.rationale: Australian routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis is 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks for D-negative women with no anti-D antibodies. (Source: NBA anti-D guidelines)

options.text: Wait for postnatal cord blood Rh D typing before giving any antenatal anti-D immunoglobulin.
options.rationale: Cord blood Rh typing guides postpartum dosing, not whether routine antenatal prophylaxis is given. (Source: NBA anti-D guidelines)

options.text: Administer Rh D immunoglobulin 250 IU intramuscularly now and a single repeat at delivery.
options.rationale: 250 IU IM is the standard for first trimester sensitising events; routine antenatal dosing is 625 IU. (Source: NBA anti-D guidelines)

options.text: Send foetal RhD genotyping on maternal plasma and defer immunoglobulin until the foetal status is known.
options.rationale: Foetal cell-free DNA RhD testing is available but not the universal Australian pathway; routine prophylaxis is still recommended. (Source: NBA anti-D guidelines)

explanation.summary: The Australian routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis schedule for D-negative women with no anti-D antibodies is 625 IU intramuscular Rh D immunoglobulin at both 28 and 34 weeks gestation. The 28 week dose targets feto-maternal haemorrhage in the third trimester before it causes sensitisation, and the 34 week dose covers the period to term. Smaller 250 IU doses are used for first trimester sensitising events such as a small antepartum haemorrhage or chorionic villus sampling.
key_points: Australian routine antenatal anti-D is 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks.
250 IU is the dose for first trimester sensitising events.
Postpartum dosing depends on cord blood Rh typing and Kleihauer testing for large feto-maternal haemorrhage.
explanation.pearls: If a sensitising event occurs after a routine 28 week dose, give an additional 625 IU within 72 hours rather than relying on the prior dose to cover both indications.

sources.label: RANZCOG statement on Rh D immunoglobulin
